title: Customs and Traditions
overview: The UK celebrates a wide variety of festivals and traditions from many different cultures and religions. This reflects the country's diverse, multicultural society. This lesson covers the main Christian festivals (Christmas, Easter), other religious festivals (Diwali, Hanukkah, Eid al-Fitr, Eid ul Adha, Vaisakhi), and other British festivals and traditions (New Year/Hogmanay, Valentine's Day, April Fool's Day, Mother's Day, Father's Day, Halloween, Bonfire Night, Remembrance Day). You will also learn about bank holidays and patron saints' days.
key_facts: Christmas Day, 25 December, celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ and is a public holiday.
Many Christians go to church on Christmas Eve (24 December) or on Christmas Day itself.
Christmas is celebrated in a traditional way with people spending the day at home eating a special meal.
A traditional Christmas meal often includes roast turkey, Christmas pudding and mince pies.
At Christmas, people give gifts, send cards and decorate their houses.
Christmas is a special time for children. Very young children believe that Father Christmas (also known as Santa Claus) brings them presents during the night before Christmas Day.
Many people decorate a tree in their home at Christmas.
Boxing Day is the day after Christmas Day and is a public holiday.
Easter takes place in March or April.
Easter marks the death of Jesus Christ on Good Friday and his rising from the dead on Easter Sunday.
Both Good Friday and the following Monday (Easter Monday) are public holidays.
The 40 days before Easter are known as Lent.
Lent is a time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter.
Traditionally, people would fast during Lent and today many people will give something up, like a favourite food.
The day before Lent starts is called Shrove Tuesday, or Pancake Day.
People eat pancakes on Shrove Tuesday, which were traditionally made to use up foods such as eggs, fat and milk before fasting.
Lent begins on Ash Wednesday.
On Ash Wednesday there are church services where Christians are marked with an ash cross on their forehead as a symbol of death and sorrow for sin.
Easter is also celebrated by people who are not religious.
Easter eggs are chocolate eggs often given as presents at Easter as a symbol of new life.
Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days.
Diwali is often called the Festival of Lights.
Diwali is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs.
Diwali celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge.
There are different stories about how Diwali came about.
There is a famous celebration of Diwali in Leicester.
Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days.
Hanukkah is to remember the Jews' struggle for religious freedom.
On each day of Hanukkah a candle is lit on a stand of eight candles called a menorah.
The menorah candles remember the story where oil that should have lasted only a day lasted for eight.
Eid al-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month.
At Eid al-Fitr, Muslims thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast.
The date when Eid al-Fitr takes place changes every year.
Muslims attend special services and meals for Eid al-Fitr.
Eid ul Adha remembers that the prophet Ibrahim was willing to sacrifice his son when God ordered him to.
Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God.
Many Muslims sacrifice an animal to eat during Eid ul Adha.
In Britain, animal sacrifice for Eid ul Adha has to be done in a slaughterhouse.
Vaisakhi (also spelled Baisakhi) is a Sikh festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa.
Vaisakhi is celebrated on 14 April each year with parades, dancing and singing.
New Year, 1 January, is a public holiday.
People usually celebrate New Year on the night of 31 December (called New Year's Eve).
In Scotland, 31 December is called Hogmanay and 2 January is also a public holiday.
For some Scottish people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than Christmas.
Valentine's Day, 14 February, is when lovers exchange cards and gifts.
Sometimes people send anonymous cards on Valentine's Day to someone they secretly admire.
April Fool's Day, 1 April, is a day when people play jokes on each other until midday.
The television and newspapers often have stories that are April Fool jokes.
Mothering Sunday (or Mother's Day) is the Sunday three weeks before Easter.
Children send cards or buy gifts for their mothers on Mother's Day.
Father's Day is the third Sunday in June.
Children send cards or buy gifts for their fathers on Father's Day.
Halloween, 31 October, is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter.
Young people will often dress up in frightening costumes to play 'trick or treat' on Halloween.
On Halloween, people give treats to stop children playing tricks on them.
A lot of people carve lanterns out of pumpkins and put a candle inside at Halloween.
Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays.
The origin of Bonfire Night was an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.
Remembrance Day, 11 November, commemorates those who died fighting for the UK and its allies.
Originally Remembrance Day commemorated the dead of the First World War, which ended on 11 November 1918.
People wear poppies on Remembrance Day (the red flower found on the battlefields of the First World War).
At 11.00 am on Remembrance Day there is a two-minute silence.
Wreaths are laid at the Cenotaph in Whitehall, London on Remembrance Day.
As well as major festivals, there are other public holidays each year called bank holidays.
Bank holidays are when banks and many other businesses are closed for the day.
Bank holidays are of no religious significance.
Bank holidays are at the beginning of May, in late May or early June, and in August.
In Northern Ireland, the anniversary of the Battle of the Boyne in July is also a public holiday.
England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint.
Each patron saint has a special day.
1 March is St David's Day, Wales.
17 March is St Patrick's Day, Northern Ireland.
23 April is St George's Day, England.
30 November is St Andrew's Day, Scotland.
Only Scotland and Northern Ireland have their patron saint's day as an official holiday.
In Scotland not all businesses and offices will close on St Andrew's Day.
Events are held across Scotland, Northern Ireland and the rest of the country on patron saints' days, especially where there are a lot of people of Scottish, Northern Irish and Irish heritage.
While the patron saints' days are no longer public holidays in England and Wales, they are still celebrated.
Parades and small festivals are held all over England and Wales on patron saints' days.
memory_hook: Think of the UK's calendar as a year-long multicultural celebration: Start with Hogmanay in Scotland (bigger than Christmas!), then Valentine's Day lovers, April Fool's jokes, Mother's Day in spring. Easter brings chocolate eggs and Lent pancakes. Summer has Father's Day and patron saints (David 1 Mar, Patrick 17 Mar, George 23 Apr, Andrew 30 Nov - remember the dates!). Autumn brings Diwali lights in Leicester, Halloween pumpkins, and Bonfire Night fireworks (Guy Fawkes 1605). November 11 at 11am: two-minute silence, poppies at the Cenotaph. Then Hanukkah menorahs, Christmas trees, and Boxing Day. Meanwhile Muslims celebrate Eid twice (end of Ramadan, and Ibrahim's sacrifice), Sikhs celebrate Vaisakhi on April 14. Bank holidays scattered throughout with no religious meaning - just days off!

questions.explanation: Christmas Day is 25 December and celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ. It is a public holiday.

options: The day after Christmas Day and is a public holiday
questions.explanation: Boxing Day is the day after Christmas Day (26 December) and is a public holiday.

questions.prompt: What is the day before Lent called?
questions.explanation: The day before Lent starts is called Shrove Tuesday, or Pancake Day. People eat pancakes which were traditionally made to use up foods before fasting.

questions.prompt: Which festival is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs and is often called the Festival of Lights?
questions.explanation: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is often called the Festival of Lights and is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs.

options: The birth of the Prophet Muhammad
The end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month
The night journey of the Prophet
questions.explanation: Eid al-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month. They thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast.
Vaisakhi is celebrated on 14 April each year with parades, dancing and singing. It celebrates the founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa.

options: The Scottish New Year celebration on 31 December
questions.explanation: In Scotland, 31 December is called Hogmanay and 2 January is also a public holiday. For some Scottish people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than Christmas.

questions.prompt: What happened on 5 November 1605 that is commemorated on Bonfire Night?
options: A group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament
The end of World War I
The coronation of Queen Elizabeth I
questions.explanation: Bonfire Night, 5 November, commemorates an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

questions.prompt: What happens at 11.00 am on Remembrance Day?
questions.explanation: At 11.00 am on Remembrance Day (11 November) there is a two-minute silence and wreaths are laid at the Cenotaph in Whitehall, London.

questions.prompt: When is St George's Day (England's patron saint)?
questions.explanation: St George's Day is 23 April. The patron saints' days are: St David (Wales, 1 March), St Patrick (Northern Ireland, 17 March), St George (England, 23 April), and St Andrew (Scotland, 30 November).

options: Public holidays of no religious significance when banks and many businesses are closed
Days when only banks are closed
questions.explanation: Bank holidays are public holidays when banks and many other businesses are closed for the day. These are of no religious significance. They are at the beginning of May, in late May or early June, and in August.

questions.prompt: What do people wear on Remembrance Day?
options: Poppies (the red flower found on WWI battlefields)
questions.explanation: People wear poppies on Remembrance Day - the red flower found on the battlefields of the First World War.

flashcards: When is Christmas Day?
25 December - celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ, is a public holiday
What is a traditional Christmas meal?
Roast turkey, Christmas pudding and mince pies
What is Boxing Day?
The day after Christmas Day (26 December), a public holiday
When is Easter?
March or April - marks the death of Jesus on Good Friday and resurrection on Easter Sunday
What is Lent?
The 40 days before Easter when Christians reflect and prepare; traditionally people fast or give something up
What is Shrove Tuesday?
Pancake Day - the day before Lent when people eat pancakes to use up eggs, fat and milk before fasting
What is Ash Wednesday?
First day of Lent - Christians are marked with ash cross on forehead as symbol of death and sorrow for sin
What are Easter eggs?
Chocolate eggs given as presents at Easter as symbol of new life
What is Diwali?
Festival of Lights (October/November, 5 days) celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs - victory of good over evil. Famous celebration in Leicester
What is Hanukkah?
Jewish festival (November/December, 8 days) remembering struggle for religious freedom - candle lit each day on menorah (8-candle stand)
What is Eid al-Fitr?
Muslim festival celebrating end of Ramadan (month of fasting) - thank Allah for strength to complete fast
What is Eid ul Adha?
Muslim festival remembering Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son - many Muslims sacrifice an animal (must be done in slaughterhouse in Britain)
What is Vaisakhi?
Sikh festival on 14 April celebrating founding of Khalsa community - parades, dancing, singing
What is Hogmanay?
Scottish New Year celebration (31 December) - 2 January also public holiday; for some Scots bigger than Christmas
When is Valentine's Day?
14 February - lovers exchange cards and gifts, sometimes anonymously
What is April Fool's Day?
1 April - people play jokes on each other until midday; TV and newspapers have April Fool stories
When is Mother's Day?
Mothering Sunday - Sunday three weeks before Easter, children give cards/gifts to mothers
When is Father's Day?
Third Sunday in June - children give cards/gifts to fathers
What is Halloween?
31 October - ancient pagan festival marking beginning of winter; young people dress in costumes for 'trick or treat', people carve pumpkin lanterns
What is Bonfire Night?
5 November - fireworks commemorate 1605 when Guy Fawkes and Catholics failed to kill Protestant king with bomb in Houses of Parliament
What is Remembrance Day?
11 November - commemorates those who died fighting for UK and allies, originally WWI which ended 11/11/1918
What happens at 11am on Remembrance Day?
Two-minute silence; wreaths laid at Cenotaph in Whitehall, London; people wear poppies (red flower from WWI battlefields)
What are bank holidays?
Public holidays of no religious significance when banks and businesses close - beginning of May, late May/early June, August (plus Battle of the Boyne in July in NI)
What are the patron saints' days?
1 March: St David (Wales), 17 March: St Patrick (NI), 23 April: St George (England), 30 November: St Andrew (Scotland)
Which patron saints' days are official holidays?
Only Scotland and Northern Ireland (though not all Scottish businesses close); England and Wales celebrate with parades and festivals but not public holidays
